questions.question: A small car collides head-on with a large truck. Which statement about the forces during the collision is correct?
options: The truck exerts a larger force on the car
The car exerts a larger force on the truck
Both vehicles exert equal forces on each other
The forces depend on which vehicle was moving faster
questions.explanation: According to Newton's Third Law, the forces are equal and opposite regardless of the mass or speed of the vehicles.

questions.question: A fly hits the windshield of a moving car. The fly is flattened, but the car's motion is unaffected. Which of the following best explains this?
options: The car exerts a larger force on the fly than the fly exerts on the car
The fly experiences a much larger change in momentum than the car
The fly's small mass means it undergoes a much larger acceleration
The car's windshield absorbs all the momentum
questions.explanation: The forces are equal (Newton's Third Law), but $a = F/m$. The fly's tiny mass results in a massive deceleration.

questions.question: A $0.5\text{ kg}$ ball traveling at $20\text{ m/s}$ strikes a wall and rebounds at $12\text{ m/s}$ in the opposite direction. What is the magnitude of the change in momentum of the ball?
questions.explanation: $\Delta p = m(v_f - v_i) = 0.5 \times (-12 - 20) = 0.5 \times (-32) = -16\text{ kg m/s}$. Magnitude $= 16\text{ kg m/s}$.

questions.question: A $2\text{ kg}$ cart and a $6\text{ kg}$ cart are initially at rest. They push off against each other. The $2\text{ kg}$ cart moves away at $9\text{ m/s}$. What is the velocity of the $6\text{ kg}$ cart?
Which of the following do NOT belong to the principle of conservation of linear momentum? (Select all that apply)
options: Total momentum of an isolated system remains constant
Momentum before an interaction equals momentum after the interaction
Momentum is conserved even when a large external force acts on the system
The total mass of a system must remain constant for momentum to be conserved
questions.explanation: C is false: external forces change momentum. D is false: mass can change (e.g., explosions) and momentum is still conserved. A and B are correct statements of the principle.

questions.question: A $1,000\text{ kg}$ car and a $2,000\text{ kg}$ truck collide. During the collision, the car experiences a force of $8,000\text{ N}$ from the truck. What force does the truck experience from the car?
options: The truck experiences no force because it is heavier
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: forces are equal and opposite. The truck experiences exactly $8,000\text{ N}$ from the car.

questions.question: During a collision between a moving billiard ball and a stationary one, the moving ball exerts a force on the stationary ball for $0.04\text{ s}$. For how long does the stationary ball exert a force on the moving ball?
options: $0\text{ s}$ (it was stationary, so it couldn't exert a force)
questions.explanation: The contact time is the same for both objects. Newton's Third Law forces act simultaneously for the same duration.

questions.question: A $1\text{ kg}$ cart moving at $10\text{ m/s}$ collides with a stationary $4\text{ kg}$ cart. They stick together and move at $2\text{ m/s}$. What is the change in velocity of each cart?
options: $1\text{ kg}$ cart: $-2\text{ m/s}$; $4\text{ kg}$ cart: $+2\text{ m/s}$
$1\text{ kg}$ cart: $-8\text{ m/s}$; $4\text{ kg}$ cart: $+2\text{ m/s}$
$1\text{ kg}$ cart: $-10\text{ m/s}$; $4\text{ kg}$ cart: $+10\text{ m/s}$
$1\text{ kg}$ cart: $-5\text{ m/s}$; $4\text{ kg}$ cart: $+5\text{ m/s}$
questions.explanation: $\Delta v_1 = 2 - 10 = -8\text{ m/s}$. $\Delta v_2 = 2 - 0 = +2\text{ m/s}$. Check: $m_1\Delta v_1 = 1 \times (-8) = -8$; $m_2\Delta v_2 = 4 \times 2 = +8$. Equal and opposite $\checkmark$.

questions.question: A person on a skateboard throws a heavy ball forward. The person rolls backward. Which of Newton's laws best explains the backward motion?
questions.explanation: The person exerts a force on the ball (action), and the ball exerts an equal and opposite force on the person (reaction), causing the backward motion.

questions.question: Two objects collide. Object A experiences an average force of $400\text{ N}$ for $0.05\text{ s}$. What is the magnitude of the impulse experienced by Object B?
questions.explanation: Impulse on A $= 400 \times 0.05 = 20\text{ N s}$. Impulse on B has the same magnitude, opposite direction.

questions.question: Two objects A and B collide head-on in a perfectly inelastic collision. A has ten times the mass of B. Before the collision, B's velocity is ten times that of A, and they move toward each other. Which object has the higher final kinetic energy?
options: Both have the same final kinetic energy
questions.explanation: Momentum of A: $p_A = 10m_B \times u_A$. Momentum of B: $p_B = m_B \times 10u_A = 10m_B \times u_A$. They have equal and opposite momentum. Total momentum = 0. After sticking together, $v_f = 0$. Both have zero final KE.

questions.question: A $70\text{ kg}$ person jumps upward from the ground at $3\text{ m/s}$. The Earth gains an equal and opposite momentum. If Earth's mass is $6 \times 10^{24}\text{ kg}$, what is Earth's change in velocity?
questions.explanation: $\Delta p_{person} = 70 \times 3 = 210\text{ kg m/s}$. $\Delta v_{Earth} = -210 / (6 \times 10^{24}) = -3.5 \times 10^{-23}\text{ m/s}$ — imperceptibly small.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered Newton's Third Law and its connection to momentum!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review why equal forces produce different accelerations for different masses.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $m_1\Delta v_1 = -m_2\Delta v_2$ and the implications of equal and opposite forces.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: action-reaction pairs, equal forces in collisions, and why impulse is the same for both objects.
